articles.title: Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel tells NBC News that he will not step down
articles.summary: Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel has told NBC News' Meet the Press that he would not step down in his first interview with a U.S. network.

articles.title: What to Know About the U.S. Military Draft Pool and Automatic Registration
articles.summary: For decades, draft-eligible men ages 18 to 25 have been required to register with the Selective Service System. Most states offer a registration option on driver’s license applications.

articles.title: Iran’s Battered Leaders Emerge From War Confident — and With New Cards
articles.summary: For Iran’s theocratic rulers, just surviving the U.S.-Israeli onslaught means victory. But the seeds of their next crisis may already be planted.

articles.title: Judge Rejects Hegseth’s Second Attempt to Restrict Reporters at Pentagon
articles.summary: A federal judge gutted a set of rules that were adopted after the court declared an earlier press policy unconstitutional, in a case brought by The New York Times.

articles.title: Amid Trump’s Threats, NATO Labors to Survive the Iran War
articles.summary: President Trump is citing the unwillingness of European nations to back the United States in the conflict as another reason to scale back or abandon the alliance. And he still wants Greenland.

articles.title: Melania Trump Says She Was Not Associated With Jeffrey Epstein
articles.summary: Responding to what she said were smears, the first lady said she never had knowledge of Jeffrey Epstein’s abuse and was not a victim of his. She called for a congressional hearing for his victims.

articles.title: Student Hit by Projectile During ‘No Kings’ Protest Lost an Eye, Lawyer Says
articles.summary: The student, Tucker Collins, 18, was observing demonstrators in Los Angeles when he was struck, the lawyer said.

articles.title: New Charter Allows RFK Jr. to Reclaim Vaccine Policy Despite Court Ruling
articles.summary: The charter, published on Thursday, alters the makeup and purpose of the panel, opening the door for Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to reclaim his revision of national vaccine policy.

articles.title: A fragile ceasefire and emboldened Iran
articles.summary: NPR's Mary Louise Kelly talks with International correspondent Daniel Estrin about how the US-Iran-Israel ceasefire is being received in Israel.

articles.title: How Trump's foreign policy is playing in North Carolina
articles.summary: What do people who follow foreign affairs make of the war with Iran and the president's foreign policy? NPR spoke with a dozen World Affairs Council members in North Carolina to find out.

articles.title: Melania Trump denies close ties to Jeffrey Epstein in rare public statement
articles.summary: The first lady made a rare public statement on Thursday saying she was not friends with the late sex offender. She also said Epstein did not introduce her to President Donald Trump.

articles.title: How did Pakistan bring Iran and the U.S. to the negotiating table?
articles.summary: Pakistan has acted as a mediator between Washington D.C. and Tehran over the last few weeks, and helped broker the ceasefire agreement this week. NPR's Juana Summers talks to Elizabeth Threlkeld, a se

articles.title: How an ancient resin traded for centuries got snarled up by the Iran war
articles.summary: It's not just energy supplies that have been disrupted by the Iran war. It's also hitting frankincense, a commodity that's been defining trade routes in the Middle East for thousands of years.

articles.title: 710,000 fewer babies were born last year in U.S. compared with two decades ago
articles.summary: The U.S. fertility rate continued its slide to historic levels, due to plunging teen pregnancies and far more women delaying motherhood into their 30s and 40s.

articles.title: How a cheese sandwich at the Masters is still $1.50 in an era of price hikes
articles.summary: The priciest concessions at the Masters, beer and wine, cost just $6 each. The Georgia golf tournament prides itself on a simple and affordable menu, even as ticket prices continue to climb.

articles.title: How bad for humans is wildlife trade? A new study has answers
articles.summary: People sell wild animals for food and for traditional medicine — legally and illegally. A study looks at the risks of spillover diseases from those pangolins, giant rats and other exotic critters.

articles.title: We can't all be astronauts, but the Artemis II crew has fitness lessons for everyone
articles.summary: The Artemis II astronauts don't have a lot of space to exercise. That's why they've got the flywheel — a small device that can be used for strength and cardio workouts.

articles.title: For Artemis II, Returning to Earth May Be the Most Dangerous Part of the Mission
articles.summary: After a successful flight around the moon, the astronauts are relying on a flawed heat shield to protect them as they re-enter Earth’s atmosphere.

articles.title: This doctor turned a 31-foot RV into one of the country's only mobile OB-GYN clinics
articles.summary: Mary Fariba Afsari's book, Labor, is a portrait of reproductive healthcare in post-Dobbs America. Her book also is about her Iranian heritage and her grandmother's death from an illegal abortion.

articles.title: In the Trump era, everybody's talking about 'soft power.' But ... what is it exactly?
articles.summary: The U.S. government long saw giving international aid as a way to build goodwill throughout the world. Did it work? And what does the reducing of foreign aid mean for that effort now?

articles.title: Iran-U.S. ceasefire off to a shaky start. And, Bill Gates to testify in Epstein probe
articles.summary: Attacks persist across the Middle East despite the two-week ceasefire deal between the U.S. and Iran. And, Bill Gates is set to testify in the investigation of the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

articles.title: Trump meets with NATO chief about the future of the partnership
articles.summary: President Trump met with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte Wednesday to discuss the future partnership after Trump disparaged the alliance.

articles.title: Israel vows to continue fighting Hezbollah but agrees to talks with Lebanon
articles.summary: Israel's prime minister said his government would begin talks with Lebanon but vowed to continue attacks against the Iran-backed Lebanese militant group Hezbollah, despite a fragile Iran ceasefire.

articles.title: Dominion still has pending lawsuits against election deniers such as Rudy Giuliani and Sidney Powell
